videos.title: Visiting Munich? Here are the Must-Knows!
videos.description:
Planning a trip to Munich? Take a look at our compact travel guide: it's packed full of useful information and tips for your stay in the Bavarian capital.

00:00 Intro
00:29 Geography – where is Munich located? 
00:55 Weather and festivities
01:47 Do's and dont's at the Oktoberfest
03:25 Must-sees in Munich
05:00 Transportation
06:00 Accommodation
06:39 Art and culture: museums
07:25 Food and drinks, Hofbräuhaus
08:00 Characteristics and short language guide

CREDITS:
Report: Elisabeth Yorck, Lukas Stege
Camera: Holm Weber
Editing: Elisabeth Yorck
Executive producer: Christina Deicke

videos.transcript: Hello there! If you want to travel to Munich, watch this video to get all the information and tips you need to make the best of it! Let's go! What are the most important sights? What makes the locals tick? And when is the best time of year to travel to Munich? In this video, I'll show you all this and much more! I'll explain the do's and don'ts of Oktoberfest and what you need to know when visiting a beer garden. But first, a few basics: Where is Munich located? Munich is the capital of the state of Bavaria and is located in the south of Germany. It's home to around 1.5 million people. From Munich, you can quickly reach the Alps where you can go hiking in summer and skiing in winter. Before we talk about the main sights, let's take a quick look at the weather. So when is the best time to come to Munich? Well, it depends on what you want to do. In summer you can walk around in shorts and a T-shirt Some days it can get really hot. In spring and fall be prepared to wear pants, sweaters and light jackets. In winter, temperatures can go below freezing and it can snow. So don't forget to pack warm clothes! I prefer to travel to Munich in the fall. Do you know why? Oktoberfest! Cheers! In September and October, the largest folk festival in the world takes place on the Theresienwiese in Munich. With more than 7 million visitors recently. There are rides, lots of food – and giant festival tents! Here are a few do's and don'ts: Wearing traditional costume is not compulsory, but you might feel left out if you don't. Women wear dirndls, men wear lederhosen. Don't overdo it with the alcohol – otherwise you'll end up on the so-called "Kotzhügel" or puke hill. Dancing on benches is allowed, but not on tables! Okay, and what about summer? There are also big events in Munich like the Korcherlball, which takes place every year in July early morning from 6 to 10 a.m. at the Chinese Tower in the English Garden. Summer is also the perfect time to explore the city's beer gardens! You can bring your own food if you only want to buy a drink. One of the city's most popular parks is the English Garden. It's also one of the largest city parks in the world. Here is a tip: watch the surfers ride the Eisbach wave. And then jump into the river yourself. The current is so strong that you will be swept way across the park. It's a lot of fun! If you travel to Munich in early winter – late November or December – you will experience the wonderful Christmas season! The city is festively decorated and you can stroll through atmospheric Christmas markets. The carnival season takes place in February with parades and street parties. Don't miss the traditional dance of the market women at the Viktualienmarkt. To visit these festivals, you are tied to specific days. But Munich's sights can be enjoyed the year-round. Here are the highlights you shouldn't miss. Marienplatz is the center of Munich's old town. Next to it is the New Town Hall. And here's a tip: get here at 11 or 12 o'clock, so you can see the carillon. Just a few steps away you'll find the Odeonsplatz with the Theatiner Church and the Feldherrnhalle loggia. Right next to it is the Munich Residenz, once the seat of the dukes, electors and kings of Bavaria from 1508 to 1918. It's well worth a visit! A bit further away but still within the city limits, you'll find the impressive Nymphenburg palace. It was the summer residence of Bavarian rulers and is one of Germany's largest palaces. More on museums later. But here's a must-see for soccer fans: the Allianz Arena, home of FC Bayern Munich, Germany's most famous soccer club. The legendary stadium holds more than 75,000 spectators. Its exterior facade has impressive lighting. If you want to go shopping, Kaufingerstraße is the place to be. It stretches from Marienplatz to Karlsplatz, also known as the Stachus. And what is the best way to get from one attraction to another? At least in the city center you can do a lot on foot. But if you want to travel a bit further, you can rent bikes or e-scooters or take public transportation. Munich has buses, streetcars, subway trains and suburban trains. The network is divided into different zones. Check beforehand where exactly you need to go so that you choose the right fare. Take a look at this. It's a little bit complicated. There is also a multi-journey ticket which gives you more flexibility. With it, you stamp two stripes for one journey. For a short trip, for example two subway stops or four stops by bus, you only need to stamp one stripe. Munich also has its own airport. It's a 40 minute S-Bahn ride from the city center. You gotta weigh it up: Is it okay for me to travel longer staying overnight in a cheap hotel outside of the city? Or do I want to stay close to the center but pay more? Munich is relatively expensive compared to other German cities. As I said, if you want it cheaper, you have to look for something further outside or stay in a hostel. Holiday apartments, campsites or couch surfing are of course also alternatives. Munich's most famous luxury hotel is the Bayerischer Hof. If you're into art, then take time to look at the Pinakotheks. They are among the most renowned art collections in the world. The Alte Pinakothek displays European paintings from the 14th to the 18th century. And the Pinakothek der Moderne is dedicated to art from the 20th century onwards. My recommendation: go to the museums on Sunday. Entry costs only 1 euro, Otherwise it will set you back about 10 euros. The Deutsches Museum is also a must. It is one of the largest science and technology museums in the world. When in Munich, it's very important to try the local cuisine! My tip: go to Munich's famous Hofbräuhaus. Here you can enjoy Bavarian cuisine and beer accompanied by Bavarian music. The food is usually hearty. You'll find snack platters, white sausages with pretzel, pork knuckle and Käsespätzle – like cheesy noodles with roasted onions. Festivals, sightseeing, food, transport, accomodation, museums – you're all set up! But what's missing? Well, the Munich people, of course! Munich embodies Bavarian coziness. This is expressed, for example, in the beer garden culture, the hearty food, traditional outfits – and of course, brass band music! The Bavarian dialect can even be difficult for some Germans to understand – Here are some Bavarian phrases to help you. I'm not from Bavaria myself, but I'll do my best! "Griaß di!" is like a Hello. "Pfiat di!" is Goodbye. But you can also say "Servus" for Hello and Goodbye – that's easier. "Hendl" is a fried chicken. "Maß" is one liter of beer. And if you're tipsy, a little bit happy, then you have a so-called "Suri". I hope all my recommendations were helpful and that you'll have a great trip to Munich! Servus, Pfiat di and Goodbye!

Travelling to Munich for the first time? In need of Munich travel advice that caters to a newbie? This Munich travel guide is packed with must-knows before you visit Munich as a tourist, with a rundown of common tourist mistakes in Munich as well as answers to common questions like Munich culture shocks, Munich culture and etiquette tips, where to go in Munich, places to visit in Munich, and more so you can have the Munich trip of your dreams. Whether you're looking for general Munich tips or tips for Munich hidden gems, be sure to watch this video!
videos.transcript: If you are visiting Munich for the first time this year, make sure you watch this video because in it, I'll be sharing all the best tips and must-knows for first-timers. Hi, I'm Christina from HappyToWander.com and I lived in Munich for several years and I honestly loved it. During the pandemic, I basically walked every single block of that city and I'd like to think I've gotten to know it really, really well, so in this video I am sharing everything, I'm sharing all my best tips and must-knows to make sure that your first trip to Munich is a roaring success, so let's get to it, let's start with some Munich travel planning tips. Now first, when should you go? Munich is obviously best known for Oktoberfest, which takes place in the fall but what a lot of visitors don't know is actually there are really fun festivals and things to do year-round, so I really do think that Munich is a worthwhile destination no matter what time of year you go. For instance, if you go in the winter you have the Christmas markets and then you have Starkbierfest, if you go in the spring you have Frühlingsfest, in the summer there's all sorts of Volks Fests and different folk festivals that take place and street festivals as well in the city, so really, I don't think that there's a bad time to go except maybe November and January, just because the weather then is a bit iffy and also there's not a ton of really interesting events happening during that period. That said, Munich is a great year-round destination, I know I'm biased but just know that if you can't make it to Oktoberfest that's not the end of the world, there's definitely other times to go that are just as worthwhile. Alright, now let's chat about where to stay. Luckily Munich is a very compact city that's well served by public transport so, so long as you stay close to an U-Bahn station you're going to be able to get to most places in the city relatively quickly. That said, if money is no object, of course, the Alstadt neighborhood, or the Old Town neighborhood, is most ideal because you're going to be within walking distance of most places. If you're looking for a more budget-friendly place to stay, often you'll find that the cheapest hotels are actually located near the main train stations. That is for a reason, of course, these areas of town aren't necessarily the nicest, although this is Munich we're talking about, it's one of the safest cities in the world in my opinion, so really, you're not going to be in any danger but just know they're not necessarily the prettiest neighborhoods but if budget is an issue this is definitely a great option, especially if you need to stay kind of in that area for events like Oktoberfest. Now besides those main neighborhoods, if you're looking to get a little bit out of the tourist core, I have a few recommendations for neighborhoods that I personally really enjoy: first of all there's Lehel, which is quiet and pretty and also, I'm biased because I used to live here, it can be quite expensive but it's very, very close to the main Town Center but it's more residential and just kind of like a nice calm area. If you're looking for something more lively and vibrant the Glockenbachviertel has a lot of different bars and restaurants, it's definitely one of the cooler areas of the city and also still very close to the touristy area in the middle; and then there's also Haidhausen which is one of my favorite neighborhoods, it's very quiet, has a villagey feel but still pretty close to the center with lots of good transport links that'll get you into the main area. There's also the area around Olympia Park which I personally love because I used to live there as well but to be honest, for first-time visitors it's not the closest to most of the main attractions, so I wouldn't necessarily choose that for first-time visitors. Now in terms of arriving in Munich, the Munich Airport, I have to say, is honestly amazing. In addition to being just a really clean, really efficient airport there's actually the world's first airport brewery located right there, so if you have the time before your flight or maybe when you arrive, definitely check out the Airbrau because they have some really great deals, the food is delicious and the beer is shockingly good and cheap for something that's at an airport. Now when you're researching flights, you might come across another airport known as Munich West Airport or more commonly, Memmingen Airport, this airport is Munich West but more like an hour and a half west, so it's actually not close to the city at all, so unless you find a really cheap deal that goes into there then I wouldn't recommend flying into that airport, there is a pretty efficient shuttle that takes you to the city but it takes an hour and a half so it's really not the quickest option. That said, a lot of really good budget-friendly airlines do fly into that airport, so if budget is a priority then maybe look into that as well. Now lastly, if you need help organizing your itinerary and time in Munich, I do have some pre-made itineraries on my website that you can steal, so be sure to check those out. Alright, now let's talk about getting around Munich and the surrounding area. First things first, public transport in Munich as I said before, is really excellent, so you don't really need to rely on renting a car or anything else. I would definitely advise against doing those Hop-On Hop-Off buses just because they're really expensive and also you can get by just fine with regular public transport, which is what the locals use and it's going to save you a ton of money as well. Now the public transport system in Munich relies on a mix of buses, trams, the U-Bahn, which is the underground metro system, and also S-Bahn, which are these trains that take you out into the suburbs. All of these are great and they also all use the same ticket, so it's really easy to mix and match public transport when you're in Munich. Now if you're not feeling super confident navigating the public transport system on your own, one really handy app to download is the MVG app and with this you can actually search up the different routes and it'll tell you exactly how you can get from point A to point B and it'll also tell you the ticket that you need and you can buy your tickets within the app and just show those if anybody comes by to check and I think that this is really handy because public transportation in Munich, there's a lot of margin for error just because there's a lot of different zones, there's a lot of different types of tickets and so if you just enter it on the app then it's really easy to have that all on your phone and then you don't have to worry about stressing, buying the right ticket. Now it's important to note that the Munich public transport system relies on an honor system, which means there's no fare gates or anything when you get there, instead they very much rely on you purchasing the right ticket, validating that ticket correctly and then showing that ticket if a ticket controller comes on board. So often, these ticket controllers will be dressed in normal clothes, yes, they go undercover and they don't reveal themselves until they're on board, so if there's a regular-looking person asking to see your ticket it's not a scam or anything, they might legitimately just be a ticket controller, so in that case make sure you hold on to them for the entirety of your journey. Now in terms of avoiding fines, you have to know it's not enough to just buy a ticket, you do have to validate it properly, so if you're getting on the U-Bahn, before you hop on board make sure you look for one of these ticket machines that you can insert your ticket in and then it'll validate for you. On the buses and on the trams there's these machines as well but they're on board, definitely make sure you validate it properly because if a ticket controller comes by and you have a ticket but you've not validated it correctly, they will still fine you and I've seen this happen actually many times, especially on the way to the airport because a lot of people will buy the wrong ticket or they'll forget to validate it, this is why I recommend using the app because once you purchase a ticket in the app then it's automatically validated and it makes things a lot easier. I've seen a lot of people incorrectly validate their tickets, especially Stripe Tickets, I would say they're a Munich specialty, it's basically this ticket you buy that has 10 stripes and then each time you use it you validate it for a set number of stripes depending on where you're going or how long you're traveling and this can be really confusing for first-timers, especially the physical ones because if you don't punch it in the right spot, they don't consider that to be validated, so this is why I recommend using the app, you can still use the app to download this type of ticket and it's a lot easier to redeem and use because it's all digital. Now besides the Stripe Ticket, there's a lot of different ways that you can save money on Munich's public transport system, over buying individual single tickets. The first one is buying a day ticket, so oftentimes you can buy a day ticket which includes unlimited journeys throughout the day, usually if you're going to be traveling at least three times throughout the day then it's going to be worth it, they also have group tickets which make things cheaper if you're going to be traveling in a group, so if you're visiting with friends or you're visiting with family, this is a great ticket to use because it tends to be a lot cheaper than buying single ones. There's also the short tickets, so if you're traveling on the U-Bahn for two stops or less or if you're traveling on the bus or tram for four stops or less, then you can buy a short ticket which is usually about half price, so that can save you a lot of money as well and if you're visiting for a longer period of time, you can also look into something known as the IsarCard and this is basically a week-long or month-long pass that gives you unlimited journeys in that time. Now if you're planning on doing a lot of day trips within Bavaria, then another ticket I can highly recommend is known as the Bayern ticket or the Bavaria ticket and this is basically a ticket that you can use for unlimited regional trains in the state of Bavaria and public transport as well, for a whole day, so this is really handy if you're doing any day trips, especially because public transport within Munich is included in this ticket and if you're traveling in a group, this can save you a ton of money just because the way the pricing works is you pay a base fee and then for each additional person you have to pay an extra six euros or something like that, so the more people you get on this ticket the cheaper it is per person. On that note, if you weren't already considering it, train travel in Bavaria is absolutely amazing and there's a lot of really great day trips that you can do from Munich, so I highly recommend you take advantage of the train system while you're there. Some of the most popular day trips include Neuschwanstein Castle, Dachau Concentration Camp and also Salzburg in Austria, which is only an hour away by train, so it's a really, really cool place known for Mozart and the Sound of Music. But besides those most popular picks, a lot of overseas visitors aren't aware of the fact that there's actually a ton of really amazing lakes really close to Munich, so if you want to do some naturey day trips, maybe going on a hike, there's tons of mountains, tons of lakes and a lot of them are accessible through S-Bahn, so you can actually save some money on the ticket by going a little bit closer. That said, one thing to be mindful of is that there are a few different train stations in Munich, so depending on where you're staying the Hauptbahnhof might not necessarily be the closest train station, just keep that one in mind. Now let's tackle some tips for Munich food and drink. So the first thing is, a lot of tourists don't realize this, when you go to a beer garden, you're actually completely free to bring your own food to consume, so a lot of the time you'll see people just buy beers, so long as you buy a drink you'll be fine and you'll often see locals bring really cute picnics, they'll put out a tablecloth and just kind of enjoy the evening, so long as you have a drink you can eat whatever food you bring, so that's a really great tip for saving money. Now on the topic of beer gardens, you should know that most beer gardens are self-service, so you'll walk in, grab your own beers, grab your own food and then put them on a tray and then head over to the kassa where they'll ring you up. Now if you notice that your total is actually way more than you anticipated to pay, the reason for that is likely because they're charging something called a pfand, or a deposit, on your glasses, so often what happens in this case is they'll give you a little token or something and then when you're done with your glasses, you can bring that token back and then they'll give you the money that you paid as your deposit back as well. And the reason it's important to keep hold of this token is sometimes they won't give you your deposit back unless you have it, it's kind of just a way to discourage people from walking around the beer garden grabbing glasses and hoarding cash, so make sure that if they give you a token, you hold on to it because you're going to need that to get your deposit money back. Now while you're in town, definitely make sure you treat yourself to a Bavarian breakfast. The traditional Bavarian breakfast consists of Weisswurst, which is a white sausage, along with some sweet mustard, usually a pretzel, as well as a wheat beer and I know the combination might not look very good it, might not sound very good but trust me, it's absolutely delicious, so well worth trying. That said, it's important you know how to eat this Weisswurst because it isn't for beginners, you know, you need to take off the skin, I've seen people eat it with the skin which isn't the nicest, so be sure to peel it first and then dip it in the sweet mustard and enjoy. Now if I were to talk about the best foods to try in Munich, this video would definitely be a million years long, so for the sake of your attention span I'm only going to highlight some of the ones that I consider quintessential must- tries but just know that beyond these there's a ton of really great foods to try in Munich, these are just the ones that for me are non-negotiable. First, roast pork, either in the form of schweinshaxe or schweinebraten, for me personally, I prefer the schweinebraten, either way, what's important is you get it with a potato dumpling and you also get it with beer gravy. It is one of the most filling and delicious meals you're ever going to have. Second is obatzda, this is like a cheese dip that they have in beer gardens, you'll use it to dip pretzels with, it's so good, definitely worth trying. Third is roast chicken, this is to me, one of the best meals to have after you've already had a few beers, it's very common at Oktoberfest and if you're not into meat, don't worry, one of my favorite Bavarian dishes is vegetarian, it's known as Käsespätzle and it's these little bouncy dumplings that are slathered in cheese and crispy onion and it's just unreal. As for dessert, there's two I really recommend. The first is Kaiserschmarrn, which is like torn, shredded pancakes topped with powdered sugar and sometimes a jam or applesauce, and then there's also Dampfnudel, which is a steamed dumpling thing that's just slathered in a vanilla sauce and I'm just drooling thinking about it. Now for one last food warning, I have to warn you these cookies are not meant to be eaten, they are very cute, they're very pretty but they are purely meant for decoration. Alright, now let's talk beer. There are six main breweries in Munich that brew within city limits and these are the only breweries that are allowed to have their beer served at Oktoberfest, so if you want to embark on a noble mission, be sure to try beer from each one of these breweries. Now unlike bars and restaurants in North America, when you walk into a restaurant in Munich you're not really going to have a choice in terms of breweries, that's just because when you go to a restaurant, usually they'll have a sign out front that tells you which brewery they serve, so when you walk in, your choice is made, like what kind of beer do you want, do you want a light beer, do you want a helles, then you just tell them that and then they'll bring you whichever brewery they serve there, so you don't have to pick between different breweries, you won't really have a choice unless you're ordering something different like a wheat beer. Trying a beer from each of the big six is, of course, a noble goal as I mentioned before, but if you're shorter on time, the one that I really recommend and is a lot of locals' favorites is Augustiner, it looks like this, it is simply delicious and they also don't really export a lot of it, so if you're gonna try it, be sure to try it here. Now if you're not into beer, don't worry, every restaurant will also have wine and different soft drinks available as well. If you're looking for some popular soft drinks two of the most popular ones are Apfelschorle, which is a sparkling apple juice, it's like half apple juice and then half sparkling water, and there's also Spezi, which is a mix of cola and also orange soda; really, really refreshing. Alright, now let's talk about Munich attractions. So the first thing you need to know is a lot of museums actually offer one euro admission on Sundays, so if you plan your visit for a Sunday you can actually visit some of the top museums in the city for super cheap and the good thing is a lot of them are located close to one another, so you can visit multiple museums in a day for the price of just two or three euros. Now if you're really into castles and palaces, one thing worth looking into is maybe getting a sightseeing pass from the Bavarian Palaces Administration. What they offer is one pass that includes admission to over 30 different castles and palaces across the state and is actually very affordable, so you can buy an annual membership for 50 euro but that includes unlimited castles and palaces in Bavaria. They also have a two-week pass, which makes more sense for most visitors, which is about 35 euro and this includes big names like Neuschwanstein Castle, the Munich Residenz and the Herrenchiemsee Palace. Now in terms of other sightseeing cards, the two main ones in Munich are the Munich Card and the Munich City Pass and I acknowledge that these are very confusing names, it's easy to get them mixed up. Now the main difference is the Munich Card is a card you pay for that gives you discounts to different attractions whereas a Munich City Pass is an all-inclusive pass you, of course, pay more for it but then it includes admission to all these different places across the city. In my opinion, the discount card doesn't really make sense unless you're really keen on doing math, I feel like it just overcomplicates things, whereas the City Pass might make sense if you're planning on doing a lot of different attractions. That said, I don't really think that it's the best value for most visitors, especially if you plan on visiting on Sunday, that's because a lot of the museums that are included in the pass are only one euro on Sundays anyway and so, unless you're planning to see a lot of different paid attractions, it might not really be worth it to get this card. Now let's get to the juicy stuff; when in Munich, of course, seeing the touristy bits is great but in my opinion, there's a lot of hidden gems that visitors miss and this is where the city really shines. So first off, there's some hidden gems in central Munich that a lot of people miss, so here are just a few: first, the Rathaus actually does do public tours and during that tour you get to see one of the most beautiful libraries in the entire world, which is the law library located in this building, it's stunning, definitely worth the see; near Stachus AKA Karlsplatz there is also a building known as the Justizpalast which, in my opinion is one of the most beautiful interiors in Munich and it's completely free to visit, you just have to go in and pass an airport-style security check, most people have no idea this is there so be sure to walk in, take a look, it's absolutely beautiful. There's also the Asamkirche which is by the Sendlinger Tor, granted this isn't really a secret anymore, a lot of people do know about it but it's this really beautiful tiny church, kind of in the city center that a lot of people just walk past, so be sure to go in and take a look; and lastly, St Michael's Church is often overlooked but it has a secret that I feel a lot of locals and visitors miss and this is the crypt at the very back of the church, which is actually the final resting place of a lot of Bavaria's most famous royals including King Ludwig II who is, of course, the king that is responsible for some of the fairy tale castles that you see around the state. Lastly, there's also a really cool alternative side to Munich that I feel a lot of visitors miss, so some of the places that you can explore to see this alternative side include the Werksviertel-Mitte, which is behind the Ostbahnhof station, it's this area that's made up of different containers, there's lots of murals, there's lots of small businesses to support and they do a lot of cool events there as well; there's also the Bahnwärter Thiel which is a super popular hangout spot, they've got lots of containers there as well and they host different events and parties, it's really cool. There's also the Alte Utting which is this old boat that they've put on top of a bridge and turned into a really cool hangout spot; and if you're into street art, there's big murals all around the city, so there's cool murals around the Hauptbahnhof area but also around Giesing, West End and in some of the tunnels kind of along the Isar River, especially under the Angel of Peace there's some cool ones, so seriously, Munich is cool, take that Berlin, we've got cool things as well. Now the view from the Olympic Tower is also really nice but for me, a really great free alternative is over at the Olympiaberg, which is this big hill in the park and you can bring a picnic and go at sunset and it's absolutely magical and totally free. And while you're there you can also visit Olympiadorf which is one of the coolest student residences in the entire world, you basically have these little bungalows that have been painted over by students and there's all sorts of cool artwork on them and I'm biased because this is actually the first place I lived in Munich, so if you want to see my work of art you can go over to Bungalow L4 but I don't live there anymore so don't go there looking for me. Alright, now let's discuss some tips for culture and etiquette. So the first thing you need to know is that Munich is the capital of the state of Bavaria and the people who live there, the Bavarians, are very proud of this fact, a lot of them consider themselves to be Bavarian first, so when you're asking questions make sure you talk about Bavarian culture, Bavarian customs and not German culture and German customs because many of them consider themselves to be quite distinct from the rest of the country and the rest of the country considers them to be quite distinct as well. Now Bavarians do have their own language but honestly, in the city, odds are nobody's going to try speaking Boirisch to you, you're going to get by just fine with some basic German phrases and also with English as well. Now in terms of greetings, "Servus" works throughout the day, so just feel free to bust that out and also know how to say thank you because you're going to say it a lot and that's very easy to remember, it's just danke. Now another important etiquette tip is to not make fun of lederhosen and dirndls, so contrary to popular belief, these are not costumes, these are actually the traditional dress of the region and so people often wear this for special occasions like weddings, for church, for special festivals, things like that, so when you see people wearing it, don't make fun of it it's actually their cultural dress and definitely don't buy costumised versions of it to wear around town because that's definitely not going to go over well. Alright, now let's move on to the last section, which is some final tips for when you arrive in Munich. First of all, don't jaywalk. I know that jaywalking is illegal in a lot of places but people still do it, in Bavaria they actually do enforce it and not only do the police enforce it, the locals enforce it as well, jaywalking is definitely not common especially when there's children present because they think that you're setting a bad example for them, so definitely don't jaywalk. Now another thing to be aware of is bike lanes. So there's a lot of bike lanes in Munich that tend to be side-by-side with the pedestrian lane, so if you're not used to bike lanes then you might accidentally walk into a bike lane very easily, so just be mindful of that fact, be sure to look on the ground for any cycling symbols or just keep an eye out for bikes so that you know you're not accidentally in their way. Wherever you go as well, make sure to bring a lot of cash and coins. While a lot of places will accept card these days, cash is still King in Munich and so having some bills on you for smaller purchases and also coins for the washroom is really important because public washrooms here, unfortunately, are not free, you do need to pay for them, as in a lot of beer halls and beer gardens there's often a bathroom attendant that's there to clean the washrooms and it's customary to leave them a small tip as well. Now another thing to be aware of once you arrive is that shops tend to be closed on Sundays, so if you need to do any shopping don't save it for a Sunday, grocery stores, retail stores, they're all closed on Sundays apart from ones that are at transportation hubs and gas stations, so if you go to a train station you will be able to find some shops that are open and the airport as well but generally speaking, just make sure you get all your shopping done not on a Sunday. Now lastly, if you want to blend in it's important for you to know that Munich locals tend to dress for the season rather than for the weather, so for me personally, when I'm getting dressed I kind of look at what the temperature of the day is and go according to that but I've noticed locals in Munich tend to more go, "Now it's officially fall so I'm going to dress for fall," or "It's officially winter, I'm gonna dress for winter," and so if you wear a dress or you wear shorts when it's fall, even if it's really warm out, you tend to get a lot of stares, I mean I personally wouldn't let it stop me, I used to just dress comfortably for myself anyway but just keep that in mind, if you want to blend in a bit more dress for the season, not for the weather. Alright, thank you so much for watching, I hope you found those tips helpful. If you have any more questions feel free to ask in the comments and for more practical travel videos just like this one be sure to like and subscribe for more. Thanks so much for watching, I'll see you guys next week - bye!

videos.transcript: Welcome to Munich, a city famous for the Oktoberfest, for beautiful and luxurious buildings. Also famous for football, cars and huge mugs of beer! The list of the best-known landmarks here in the Bavarian capital is breathtaking: We have the Munich Residenz, many public parks and gardens. The lovely English Garden for example, has 900 acres for nature lovers! Munich also has many renowned museums for history and art enthusiasts. There are many must-see places here, but Munich is a big and multi-faceted city. So we are looking for places a little bit outside of the radars of most tourists. So today we are taking you to see some hidden gems that are lesser-known but very worth visiting. In this video we will eat some unusual food in unusual places and explore some street art, remember WWII scars, visit an odd museum and a farmer's market and watch people surf on a river. To start our day, we are here at Marienplatz, the center and heart of the city, and events such as the famous Christmas market, cultural festivals and sports events all happen right here all year 'round. Here you can see well-known sights such as the Old City Hall, the Fischbrunnen and the magnificent New City Hall with its historic Glockenspiel. Every day the Glockenspiel shows two events from Munich's history: The first is the wedding of Duke Wilhelm V and Renate of Lorraine, the second is the Schäffler dance remembering the barrel makers who are said to have been the first to venture out into the streets again dancing to amuse the population after a severe plague epidemic. The show starts daily at 11a.m., with additional shows at noon and 5p.m. in summertime. This is the perfect place to start your visit to the Bavarian capital since many attractions are just a stone throw away and our next stop is just around the corner. When the city's food market grew too large for the Marienplatz, King Maximilian I issued a decree saying that the new market should be just a few meters away to the southeast and then Viktualienmarkt was born. Over the years, the market has evolved from just a farmer's market to a popular place to buy fresh food. Here you can find flowers, plants, fruits, vegetables, chicken, eggs, butter, honey, fish, meat, sausages, herbs and even souvenirs if you need them. And even though this market is very famous, everyone knows about it, everyone that comes to Munich comes here. We are going to do something different – for us! The breakfast here is very traditional for the Bavarian people, but not for us. Yes, it's not everyday that we drink a beer in the morning, but we must follow tradition! The Weisswurst Frühstück is the traditional Bavarian breakfast, includes a couple of sausages, a pretzel, and a refreshing Weissbier. Weisswurst, translated as white sausage, is a mix of finely minced veal and fresh pork bacon. There are two ways of eating this white sausage: You can remove the skin by cutting the sausage lengthwise and then rolling the meat from the skin with a fork. That's the way I will do it because it's the most simple. But if you want to try to be a true Bavarian, you can try the traditional informal way called 'zuzeln'. This way you basically cut each end of the sausage and you suck the meat out of the skin. But I think this is too much for me. In the end just remember to avoid eating the skin unless you want to give yourself away as a confused tourist! You can enjoy a Weisswurst breakfast in almost every Bavarian restaurant, usually between 9 and 11 a.m. Since tradition states that you can not eat Weisswurst after 12 p.m. There is even a saying that states that these sausages should hear the churches bells noon chime. Speaking of church bells, while we are still in the old town, we are going to visit the most rococo church we ever saw in our whole lives, the Asamkirche, right behind me. Officially called the Church of Saint Johann Nepomuk, this church is better known as the Asamkirche or Asam Church in honor of its builders, the Asam brothers. Egid Quirin Asam and Cosmas Damian Asam were Baroque masters in the 18th century when they built this place as their own private chapel. The Asam Church has a facade width of only 8 meters and a depth of 22 meters. And with its excessive amount of ornamentation and detail in such a small area, the interior of the church is quite overwhelming. If you look closely among the gold mosaics, and frescoes you can see a sculpture near the entrance as a friendly reminder of our finite experience on earth: a skeletal image of death about to cut the life-thread of a soul with its scissors. And since we are speaking about special churches, this is Munich's most famous one: the cathedral Church of Our Lady, the Frauenkirche. I'm sure you have seen it before on postcards: its two towers measure just under 100 meters and you can see it towering over the skyline of the city. Highlights include the south tower which is open to visitors and offers a view of the entire city and inside you can see the so-called 'Devil's Footstep'. There are many versions of the famous legend of the Devil's footprint. According to one of the best known versions, this famous footprint here in the entrance hall was made by the devil himself. In the 15th century architect Jorg von Halspach went looking for money to build a new cathedral in Munich and ended up making a bargain with the Devil: he would provide the funds for a huge building, but it had to be built with no windows, in total darkness. When the building was complete, the Devil came inside to survey the architect's work and at first, he could not see any windows, as they were covered by the columns. But then when he took another step further, the columns that had been blocking the view of the windows opened up and, in his fury at being tricked, he stamped his foot, forever marking the floor with his black footprint. Angrily, he turned into wind in order to destroy the building. And people say he is still trying to this day. And everytime we come here we can feel the winds around the church, and mostly here by the door everytime is a certainity we are going to feel some wind. Moving on we reach the last church of our itinerary: it's an Augustinian Abbey built in the 13th century and since 1966 it's the house of the German Hunting and Fishing Museum. Originally opened in 1938, this museum was created in response to a public demand because of the area's long tradition of game hunting. This unusual museum houses over 1,000 taxidermy animals, some of them quite rare. I know this museum can sound a little bit too creepy, at least for me, maybe. But they are really educational at some pont. So there are lot of informations about the fishes and about the animals. And it's about history, too and the traditions here. So it is interesting and also there is this beautiful building that you can see. But if rare animals are not enough for you, this museum also features a permanent exhibit on a very special mythical creature. In German folklore, the Wolpertinger is an animal said to inhabit the alpine forests in Bavaria. The exact origin of the myth is unclear, all we know is that in the 19th-century taxidermists began assembling specimens from body parts of different animal species in order to sell them to gullible tourists. And starting today I'll be on the lookout for Wolpertingers on our walks through Munich's gardens and forests. One of the most beautiful places we can visit here in Munich is definitely the Court Garden by the Residenz Palace and it's even better on such a nice spring day like today. But unfortunately, things were not always beautiful around here. With Munich's rich history come the scars from conflict and the war. And this is evident here on the walls of the old library of the Ludwig Maximilian University of Munich where we can see the Wounds of Memory. 50 years after the end of World War II, the artists created it as a memorial to the horrors of war, drawing attention to the holes made by bombshells and grenades that are still visible on streets and squares, buildings and works of art around Europe. Here at this wall, we can see huge holes and they are covered by this glass, which is a way to preserve the holes and also let us see through it. And these delicate pieces of glass are at huge contrast with the horrors that happened here. But another sharp contrast present in Munich's history is the story of heroism in the face of the brutal oppression of the Nazi regime. And we have a monument to it right here at the University. Here we can see some replicas of the White Rose leaflets scattered across the sidewalk, these pamphlets immortalize some remarkable young Germans that opposed the nazi regime. The White Rose was a resistance movement founded right here in the University of Munich in 1942. It was started by Sophie and Hans Scholl and their friend Alexander Schmorell, all three students at the University. all three students at the University. When the war started they were forced to interrupt their studies to attend the compulsory national service and were sent to the Eastern front. During this time, they learned of the atrocities being committed by the Nazis and, horrified by the tyranny, they decided to form a nonviolent resistance movement to inform all civilians about these crimes through anonymous leaflets and graffiti. Today the Scholl simblings are probably the most well known German activists against the nazi regime during WW2. There are several monuments dedicated to the White Rose movement throughout Munich, many of them around the University itself. Another memorial worth mentioning can be found here at Viscardigasse, also known as the Deserter's Alley. The failed coup attempt by the Nazi Party in 1923 at the Feldherrnhalle is called the Beer Hall Putsch and resulted in the deaths of four Bavarian policemen and 16 Nazi soldiers in addition to Hitler's arrest for treason. Once Hitler came to power, the site of Feldherrnhalle was declared to be a national monument. And it was made mandatory for all passers-by to perform the Hitlergruss, the Nazi salute. At this location where officers were stationed to ensure this happened. However not everyone supported Hitler or the Nazi party, and this alley provided an alternate route around the monument and away from the officer's eyes for an act of resistance. Today this little alley is a pedestrian-only walkway with a trail of bronzed paving bricks in honor of all the Germans that refused to give Hitler even a salute. Speaking of the Beer Hall Putsch, it is a common misconception to think it was here, at the Hofbräuhaus. Maybe people think that because Hitler was actually here, but it was to give the foundational speech for the nazi party. But long before any of that Duke Wilhelm V decided to build his own brewery and soon all Munich innkeepers were serving Hofbräu beer. In 1828 Bavarian King Ludwig I made the pub open to all, and the public was allowed into the Hofbräuhaus, a place that nowadays can seat more than 3000 people at once to eat and drink typical Bavarian specialties. And even if it's lunchtime already, we are not going to be one of those 3000 people.. Remember: we are trying to do a little alternative tour here. So we are going to take the train, it's about 300 meters from here, and we are going to go to the south of the city to check this interesting place that is in an old railway station. So, let's go! In 1890, Kaiser Wilhelm had the Isar Valley Railway built as a major project along the river Isar. And for 80 years the Grosshesseloher station served travelers but eventually the station was scrapped in the 1970s and was then bought in the 1980s to become a brewery. I got some pork medallions with bacon, Spätzle and a cream sauce that is like the house cream sauce. And they also have other Bavarian classics that you can try. For example the Schnitzel, they have special Schnitzels here, with a couple of options. And if you are very hungry they do not have the pork knuckles that are also very searched for, very well known. But they do have like half a duck if you are really hungry, want one of those big-big portions, I think that's your option then. My favorite vegetarian dish here in Germany is the Spätzle, and this one is a Käsespatzle, with cheese. This cheese is a very traditional Bavarian one. Spätzle is kind of like if spaghetti had a lovechild with a gnocchi. You know, it's like fluffy, but it's also kind of like pasta. It is delicious, you have to try it if you have the opportunity. We finished our meal, it was very delicious. But also the portions here, in southern Germany, are quite massive. I would take a nap to be honest, but instead we are going to take a nice walk. And we saw that we are very close to the river, so that's our plan. The river Isar cuts Munich in two and all along its shores there are many pathways full of greenery so you can follow the waters and really enjoy the day. We see people cycling, walking their dogs or just relaxing by the water. It's a very idyllic place to relax, especially after a very big meal. When we talk about alternative places to go in big cities, usually people think about graffiti and street art. But here in Munich this is not a very strong force. If you want to see some very good graffiti and street art you need to look for it. But if you take a bike or go for a walk following the river you can find a lot of very beautiful graffiti like we are seeing here. This is a tunnel right under the Luitpoldbrücke and here we can see a lot of very beautiful graffiti, pieces of art. Another wonderful place of greenery to visit in the middle of Munich is the English Garden. It's basically the city's central park in a way. You can do many things here, you can just chill, relax, or maybe you can drink a beer by the Chinese tower. You can play volleyball, you can play football, and even a very usual sport that you would not expect to see in a big city very, very far away from the coast. The Eisbach river was man made to go through the English Garden and its waters come from the Isar river, a much larger river. So that's why the stream is so constant and so fast as well, and that makes for very good waves. And that's what they did here in the 70's! What is a wave if not water hitting the floor! Right? I'm no engineer, but engineers actually did it. So they manage to get this constant wave, that is basically endless, 24/7. And people use this, 24/7, all year around. Even in winter! It is known as the Eisbachwelle, so the wave of the Eisbach river. And it does attract surfers from all over the world, and even inspired other cities to do the same. As a non-engineer it's very surprising how people can just build waves like that. And it works very well. If you are watching this video you probably know a thing or two about Germany and Munich and tourism over here. So you probably already knew about the Eisbachwelle. Something you probably didn't know that here we have the Kleine Eisbachwelle, another wave you can surf right here in the heart of Munich. We call it "Kleine" - the small one - because the water is not so fast. So it's a little bit easier to surf here. I would disagree maybe, because it looks like it's as difficult as the other one, but: what do I know? It was a long way along the Isar river, it was a long day exploring Munich, so we are going to relax now and enjoy our big Mass of beer! If you remember, at the beginning of this video, we talked about huge mugs of beer, we also talked about a lot of alternative stuff. This is very traditional, this is not alternative at all, but some things are traditional for a reason and you shouldn't miss! So: Ein Prosit! And tchüss! And if you enjoyed exploring Munich with us, leave a like in this video, and also don't forget to subscribe to DW Travel!

videos.transcript: I'm at Oktoberfest in Munich, the world's biggest folk festival, full of beer, cheer and Bavarian tradition. But there's also a dark side. I'll be uncovering these hidden dangers and share the best ways to stay safe and ensure a successful Oktoberfest. Every year over 6 million people from around the world come to the Theresienwiese for the massive steins of beer, pretzels as big as your head and a feel good party atmosphere. There's fun times for everyone. What's your favorite thing to do here at Oktoberfest? Drink. I love to drink beer. I think drinking beer in a tent. Drink. Of course, for many, it's all about the beer. Last year, 6.5 million litres of beer were served. With alcohol percentage starting at 5.8. The heavy drinking can lead to some unpleasant situations. The Bavaria statue on the grounds has certainly seen some things. One infamous example is the notorious Kotzhügel or puke hill. That's where intoxicated festival-goers often end up. To avoid any unfortunate encounters, it’s best to steer clear. The place is full of pickpockets, vomit and those who have exceeded their drinking limits. That's why urgent medical services are crucial here. At the Oktoberfest Service Center there's a fully equipped clinic with medical staff on standby and even an intensive care unit ready for emergencies. Peter Aicher, the founder of Ambulance Aicher, shows me the operation center and some other features that are necessary at Oktoberfest. Besides sobering up drunk people, they also see falls, head injuries, cuts and open wounds, heart attacks and much more. Since 2022, they even have a CT scanner to quickly see if a patient needs to be sent to the hospital. You can always reach emergency services by dialing 112 or call the police at 110. Last year, in 2023, there were over 1,800 police involvements including physical assaults, fights with beer steins, bag thefts, assaults on police officers, and drunk driving. And there are even more sobering reports. With the alcohol fueled atmosphere there is an air of 'anything goes' and women are often sexualized. It is unfortunately not uncommon to hear of sexual assaults, harassment, upskirting and drink spiking incidents. In 2023, there were 73 reported sex crimes, among them six rapes, according to police reports. There may have been more cases that went unreported. Just like anywhere else, women often have to stay aware of their surroundings. You have to be careful. You have to be very aware of your situation. You know, there's a lot drunk and people's boundaries disappear. So women do need to be careful of that. I think a lot of people, when they get drunk, they change a little bit. It can be a little bit dangerous in a way that maybe some people are getting more touchy. You know, people like to touch people. So yeah, take care of yourself. There are several initiatives focused on women's safety and respect in general. Such as the WiesnGentleman campaign. In addition to raising awareness on site, they give out the Wiesn Courage Award to those who step up to help others. Festival-goers can submit stories of civil courage online to be nominated for prizes. There's also a safe space for women by the Sichere Wiesn campaign, which you'll find in the service center. Women and girls can seek assistance if they feel lost or unsafe. They can charge their phones, file police reports, get counseling in several languages, and even get taxi vouchers to get home safe. The safe space can be found at the service center and is open daily from 6 pm to 1 am and from 3 pm on Fridays, Saturdays, and on October 2nd and 3rd. Last year, they helped over 300 women and girls. There's also the Safe Now app, available to use in two tents: the Schottenhamel and the Armbrustschützenzelt. It lets you quickly activate an alarm to emergency services, friends and family whenever you feel unsafe. And what other safety tips do people have here? I think it's a good thing to go with friends. The later the evening goes on make sure you go to the toilet together. Don't bring a huge bag. Don't search for trouble. Don't look for trouble. Between every beer you have to drink one water. Water! It's the best thing you can do. The safety campaigns also suggest having a meeting point with your group and handwriting important phone numbers in case your phone dies. Another well known tip is to wear bike shorts underneath your dirndl. Especially if you want to hop on the classic Teufelsrad ride. While the responsibility shouldn't just be on women, wearing shorts is a practical tip, adding protection against unwanted glances and letting you enjoy the thrill with confidence. So yes, come and join in all the festival fun at Oktoberfest – but by practicing good safety and being aware, you'll make sure the good times last. If you like this video, subscribe to see more at DW Travel.

videos.transcript: Planning a trip to Munich? This video will cover everything you need to know. I'll break down this incredible city and show you where to go, what to eat, and where to stay. After that, I leave you with a few insider tips that will make your visit a little easier. Welcome to Munich, the charming capital of Bavaria and Germany's third largest city. This vibrant metropolis offers a delightful blend of history, culture, and modern amenities that will captivate any traveler. So, grab a pint and let's dive into the historical city of Munich. Best times to visit. The best time to visit Munich depends on what you're looking for. September and October bring the world famous October Fest where you can indulge in Bavarian beer and hearty food. If you prefer a quieter time, spring, April to June, offers pleasant weather and blooming gardens. Winter transforms the city into a festive wonderland with its renowned Christmas markets, while summer is perfect for enjoying the city's beer gardens and outdoor attractions. Getting to and around Munich. Munich is one of Germany's most accessible cities. Well connected by air, rail, and road. Munich International Airport, the country's second busiest, lies about 40 minutes from the city center. The easiest way into town from Munich International Airport, is by Esban. Either the S1 or S8. Trains depart every 10 minutes, alternating between the two, so you'll rarely wait more than 5 minutes. If you're arriving by train from another city, Munich's Hbanhoff is one of Europe's busiest rail hubs with connections to cities across Germany and beyond. Long-d distanceance buses also serve Munich from across Europe. Munich's public transport is efficient and easy to use. The Uban, underground, Esban, suburban trains, trams, and buses cover the entire city. Cycling and scooters is also a fantastic way to explore Munich as it has dedicated bike lanes. Taxis and ride sharing services like Uber are available, but can be the most expensive to get from A to B. Munich is a very walkable city and is the best way to get around Munich, especially if the weather is nice. To make your life easier, download both the MVV app and DB Navigator app. The MVV app is your go-to tool for all local transport in Munich, including the Uban, ESBAN, trams, buses, and even regional trains within the Munich zone. It offers realtime route planning, updates on delays or detours, and lets you buy tickets directly in the app. Single rides, day passes, group tickets, airport fairs, you name it. The DB Navigator app, Deutsche Barn, is your tool for long-d distanceance and nationwide train travel. It covers all of Germany and even trips into neighboring countries like Austria and Switzerland. You'll use it for booking trains like the ICE IC and EC, viewing seat reservations, platform numbers, coach locations, and for traveling between cities like Munich to Berlin. It also shows local transit in many cities including Munich, so it can double as a backup to the MVV app. Both apps allow you to buy tickets with a credit card, PayPal, Google, and Apple Pay. Tickets are stored as digital QR codes. No printing or paper needed. If you're just sightseeing, hop on hop off buses are available in Munich and are a fun way to hit major landmarks. Prices range from 20 to30 depending on the route and duration 24 hours or 48 hours passes. But honestly, public transport covers all the same ground for a fraction of the price. And with a day pass around €8.80 for inner city zones, you can explore freely without waiting for the next tour bus. Munich's layout. Munich is divided into 25 burrows. Each of the burrows have their own character and attractions. There are over 1 and a2 million people within the city of Munich. It's comparable in population to the city of Philadelphia, Pennsylvania. For this video, I will cover Oldtown and the surrounding area. This is where you will spend the bulk of your time. At the center of Oldtown is Maran Plats, Munich's main square and beating heart, flanked by the new town hall, whose glocken spiel clock tower draws crowds daily with its life-sized chiming figurines reenacting Bavarian tales. Marian Platz is named after Marian Soiler, the Virgin Mary perched high to help guard over the city. Just a short stroll away is the Fraen Kersher, Munich's iconic twin domed cathedral. Stop inside to admire her towering ceilings and find out why the devil's footprint is stamped into the floor. Across from the Glockenville, St. Peter's Church is Munich's oldest Catholic church. Climb the tower for rewarding panoramic views of the city and the Alps on a clear day. For a local flavor, head to Victualian Mart, a bustling open air market full of gourmet foods, local produce, and a charming beer garden shaded by chestnut trees. Now, a quick crash course on beer garden etiquette because yes, there is a system. If you see a tablecloth on the table, that's the full service zone. A waiter will come by, take your order, and you can sit back and relax. Bare tables, on the other hand, mean selfservice. You grab your own food or bring your own picnic, but you're expected to buy your beer from the garden itself. No sneaking in a six-pack. Beer gardens are also prime spots to dive into some hearty Bavarian comfort food. Don't miss the tasty curry worst, a Bavarian pretzel with oats or a labor cassml. Basically, a hot meatloaf sandwich with mustard. And yes, it's better than it sounds. A liter of top-notch German beer will cost around €10 with a half liter clocking in at about €5. Most beer gardens open around 10:00 a.m. and wind down by 6 or 700 p.m. So get in early, stake out a shady spot, and enjoy the good life Munich style. Moving on. If you're into ornate interiors or just need a pick for the gram, don't miss Asam Kisha, a tiny but mind-blowing Barack church tucked into a side street. Another historic gem is St. Michael's Church, a grand renaissance structure that houses the tomb of King Ludvig II. Just north of Oldtown is the Englisher Garden. It's one of the largest urban parks in Europe, bigger than New York's central park. Within the garden, there are many walking and biking trails, as well as plenty of room to spread out and enjoy the day. The Chinese Tower Beer Garden is a favorite for enjoying a cold beer in the shade. If you're looking for something a little more offbeat, head over to the southeast corner of the park to the iceback stream where locals surf a stationary wave year round, river surfing in the middle of a European city. Tucked into one of Munich's oldest buildings is the October Fest Museum, offering a fun dive into the world's most famous beer festival. Although the museum isn't very big, there is a cozy bar on the first floor where you can sample different beers. The Munich residence was the opulent seat of power for Bavarian monarchs from the 14th century until 1918, and today it stands as one of Europe's most extensive palace complexes. It's a time capsule of Bavarian ambition, artistry, and authority. Just outside the residence lies Odian Splatz, a grand square framed by neocclassical architecture and steeped in history. On its southern end stands the Feld Herrenhala, a monumental lodia honoring Bavarian generals. To the right rises the beautiful Theotine Church with its striking yellow barack facade and rich interior that once served as a royal church. But Odon Plats also carries darker memories. It was the site of the failed 1923 beer hall push, Adolf Hitler's early attempt to seize power during which several Nazi supporters and police officers were killed in a street clash at the Feldhan Hala. In the aftermath, Hitler turned the site into a Nazi shrine, mandating that passers by give a salute. Some locals resisted by detouring through a narrow passageway called Biscardigasa, nicknamed Shurker's Alley. A subtle line of golden cobblestones now runs along the alley, commemorating those who chose conscience over compliance. Now, let's talk about something essential. Traditional German food, specifically the kind that fills your soul and your stomach. You'll find these classic Bavarian dishes at Munich's many beloved beer halls scattered throughout Oldtown, most of which double as full service restaurants. Start your culinary journey the traditional way with vice vers. Pair it with a warm chewy Bavarian pretzel and a tall glass of waste beer. A slightly sweet wheat beer that yes is considered a perfectly acceptable breakfast drink in Bavaria. By lunch or dinner, you'll want to sink your teeth into heartier affair like Schwin Shacks crispy pork knuckle. Schwiner Bratton roast pork with crackling or the regional twist on a classic Munchna schnitle which is lightly coated in mustard and horseradish before being fried to perfection. On the side, don't miss the cheesy noodle-like quesets along with other comforting staples like sauerkraut, potato dumplings, and red cabbage. No Bavarian feast is complete without dessert. Try the warm, flaky apple strudel filled with cinnamon apples. Or dig into the fluffy chaos of Kaiser Man, a shredded pancake dusted with powdered sugar and served with fruit compost. And if you're craving something fried and sweet, head to Cafe Fresh Hoot for a schmelds noodle, a deep fried dough pastry that's dangerously addictive. If you only visit one beer hall, make it the world famous Huffroy House. It's not just Munich's most iconic beer hall. It's a piece of living history. During the Nazi era, this very building was used for political rallies. And while the swastikas once painted on the ceiling have since been covered with decorative pin wheels, the past still echoes in its massive halls. Walk through the courtyard, head upstairs, and you'll find the grand chamber where Hitler once tried to stir support. Today, thankfully, it's filled with clinking steines, music, and tourists enjoying a more joyful version of history. As for where to stay, Munich caters to every type of traveler. For a luxury experience, Irish Hof delivers with elegant rooms and a rooftop terrace that serves up jaw-dropping views of the city. Just as refined, Hotel Via Yaris Sitan Kempinsky offers fivestar comfort right in the heart of town. If you're looking for something more affordable without sacrificing location or charm, both Hotel Schlicker and the Platzel Hotel are excellent mid-range picks. And for those on a budget, Euro Youth Hostel is a backpacker favorite. While Wombat's City Hostel stands out for its social atmosphere and clean, modern vibes. While this video didn't dive into museums, I've got you covered. This is a list of the best museums in and around Munich. And if you're planning to venture beyond the city, don't miss my video on the best day trips from Munich linked in the description.

videos.transcript: As a tour guide I want to show you the best Munich itinerary, so you can visit all of its many famous attractions in just a single day or in two inspiring days. If you want to start early it makes sense to begin in the old town with Munich Cathedral as it is one of the first places to be open in the morning. Impressive Church with a rich history dating back to the 15th century is recognizable by its twin towers with unique onion-shaped domes. They rise for 99 meters or 325 feet and are the highest structures in the old town. The south tower can be visited as it offers a splendid panoramic view, but it opens only at 10 am. Church interior is a simple, vast space split by a double row of 22 high, slender columns supporting the vaulted ceilings. Awe inspiring space boasts beautiful stained glass windows and beautiful artwork. Also look for the black footprint of the Devil embedded in the floor and take a moment to hear the captivating tale behind it. The fastest way to move around the old town is to walk. Only a 5 minute walk from the cathedral will get you to a small but significant square right next to the Royal Palace. Its architecture was inspired by the Italian Renaissance as it even has a monumental loggia, that is a direct replica of the one in Florence. The square represents the end of a grand boulevard that stretches between the Royal Palace and Victory Gate, connecting key institutions, government buildings and architectural landmarks. One of them is a beautiful church of King Ludwig who commissioned this avenue and square. From the square you can enter to one of Europe's most significant and the largest palace complexes. For centuries it was used as the royal residence by the Bavarian monarchs and evolved from a medieval castle into a massive palace complex. The interior was reconstructed and refurbished to its original state to reflect the power and wealth of the Bavarian rulers. Besides visiting the main residence used as a seat of government, you can also visit several museums. They are showcasing an impressive collection of art, artifacts, and historical treasures. But for me the most impressive one is a Treasury museum with a collection of Royal jewels spanning over 1000 years and it shouldn’t be missed. After a splendid time in the interior you will want to catch some fresh air in the nearby English Garden Park, one of the largest urban parks in Europe. One of the park's unique attractions is the Eisbach, a small man-made river that flows through the park. River provides consistent waves that attract surfers and can be surfed year-round. Unique and popular urban river surfing scene is a fascinating spectacle as many people gather along the riverbank to watch the surfers in action. By now you will probably need some food and drinks, and not far away is the best place to cater both needs. One of the most famous beer halls in the world, an iconic Hofbräuhaus serves delicious beer and must try traditional Bavarian dishes. As Munich is world famous for its Oktoberfest and beer gardens, this brewery is simply a must visit as it is considered to be the cradle of Bavarian beer culture. Its historical rooms are filled with a lively atmosphere, often featuring live Bavarian music and dance. Its 100 years old tables are reserved for the regulars who are drinking from personalised beer mugs stored in a beer mug safe shelves. Short walk from the Hofbrauhaus is the very heart of Munich, its main square. I love this lively and vibrant square full of the hustle and bustle of daily life. The most impressive among its historical buildings is the neo-Gothic New Town Hall with a popular Glockenspiel. A mechanical clock with figurines performs every day at 11 am, at noon and at 5 pm and the show lasts little over 10 minutes. At the center of the square stands a tall column topped by a golden statue of the Virgin Mary, an iconic symbol of Munich. If you have the time for a deep dive, wait for the Glockenspiel show in one of the surrounding bars to soak up the atmosphere. Literally a few steps from the Main square is one of the best and unique vantage points in Munich. St. Peter's Church is the oldest parish church in Munich famous for its ceiling fresco and a baroque main altar with a golden figure of St. Peter. But besides exploring the interior, you should climb its bell tower nicknamed Old Peter. Due to its location in the very heart of the Old Town and height of 91 meters or 300 feet, the tower's viewing platform will reward you with one of the best views and some of the most iconic landmarks of Munich right in front of you. But first you will need to climb its 306 steps. Cross the main square and look for one of the most famous and vibrant shopping streets in Munich, Kaufingerstrasse. As its name suggests this pedestrian-only street has a wide range of retail stores, boutiques, and department stores. It evolved from a simple medieval trading route into a sophisticated and cosmopolitan street, yet it managed to retain its historic charm. The street is always buzzing and ends with a medieval arched gate at Karlsplatz square, one of the transport hubs of Munich. From here you can hop to the underground to get to the headquarters of world famous car and motorcycle brand BMW. The complex is marked by a distinctive skyscraper and futuristic architectures of BMW Museum and BMW World. A famous circular building holds a museum showing exhibits of cars, motorbikes, engines and various design projects from more than 100 years of the company's history. Next to it is the latest masterpiece of modern architecture, BMW World where you can see the latest and the greatest cars and motorbikes of the entire BMW group. From the BMW World crossing the pedestrian bridge over the major road is the Olympic Park, built as the main venue for the 1972 Summer Olympics. Park boasts remarkable modernist architecture including an iconic Olympic stadium, beautifully landscaped gardens, open spaces, and a serene lake. The park hosts numerous events and concerts throughout the year. Be sure to visit one of the highest towers in Europe, the TV tower also known as Olympic Tower. An elevator will take you to an impressive viewing platform 192 meters or 630 feet above ground. Go back to the underground and find your way to the outskirts of Munich where you’ll find a magnificent summer residence of the Bavarian monarchs. Vast palace complex with huge and beautifully landscaped gardens showcases exquisite Baroque and Rococo architecture. Palace with ornate facades and grand halls offers lavishly decorated rooms and interesting museums. One of them you shouldn’t miss holds a fascinating collection of historical carriages and sledges used by the Bavarian royalty. The palace is surrounded by a huge park, filled with fountains, lakes and beautiful pavilions where you can spend a lot of time. Be aware that the timings in this itinerary don’t include the time spent in queues. If you have just one day in Munich it is possible to visit all attractions in one very long summer day following my Quick Visit itinerary. On the other hand, the Deep Dive itinerary allows you more time to properly explore the sites and requires at least 2 full days. If you have extra time you should also do a Full-Day Trip to picturesque and world famous Neuschwanstein and Linderhof castles at the foothills of the Alps. You will find that trip along with my other favorite Munich Experiences in the description below, or by simply scanning the QR code on the screen. But before you go to Munich you should also watch this video.
